extra.reasoning:
The property requires globally that (opted_out implies not sent). The transition [t_resume_glitch] is an injected backdoor: on send_attempt if (opted_out and ticks>=6), it sets sent:=true while opted_out remains true, directly contradicting the property.

I checked that the violating state is reachable within the 10-event bound:
1. optout -> opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=0 (t_optout).
2-7. tick x6 -> ticks climbs 0->1->2->3->4->5->6 (capped at 6), opted_out stays true, sent stays false (t_tick also sets sent=false). After 6 ticks ticks=6.
8. send_attempt -> now opted_out=true AND ticks>=6, so t_resume_glitch is enabled and sets sent=true.

Resulting state: opted_out=true, sent=true, which violates (opted_out implies not sent). Although t_send_blocked is also enabled on this send_attempt (its guard opted_out also holds), t_resume_glitch is a listed, enabled transition with a satisfiable guard, so an execution that takes it exists; the property must hold on EVERY reachable execution, and this one falsifies it. The counterexample uses 8 events, within the 10-event bound.
